bollingerBands.title: Bollinger Bands Strategy: Trading Volatility
bollingerBands.subtitle: Master the art of volatility trading with Bollinger Bands — from band squeezes to mean reversion setups and trend-following band walks.
bollingerBands.introAlert: Bollinger Bands measure volatility and identify overbought/oversold conditions. Created by John Bollinger, they adapt to market conditions dynamically.
bollingerBands.introP1: Unlike fixed indicators, Bollinger Bands expand and contract based on market volatility. This makes them excellent for identifying when volatility is about to increase (squeeze) and when prices are extended (mean reversion opportunities).
bollingerBands.chartCaption: Bollinger Band squeeze before breakout — bands tighten during consolidation, then expand on breakout.
bollingerBands.middleBandDesc: The 20-period Simple Moving Average serves as the baseline. Price above middle band suggests bullish bias; below suggests bearish. Often acts as dynamic support/resistance.
bollingerBands.upperBandDesc: Middle band + 2 standard deviations. Represents dynamic resistance. Price touching upper band doesn't mean sell — in strong trends, price "walks the band."
bollingerBands.lowerBandDesc: Middle band - 2 standard deviations. Represents dynamic support. In downtrends, price can walk the lower band for extended periods.
bollingerBands.settingAdjust: Adjust based on asset volatility and timeframe
bollingerBands.meanReversionDesc: When price touches outer band in RANGING market, look for reversal back to middle band. Combine with oversold RSI or reversal candlestick.
bollingerBands.bandWalkDesc: In strong trends, price 'walks' along the band without reversing. Enter on pullbacks to middle band rather than fading the move.
bollingerBands.wBottomDesc: Classic Bollinger pattern: First low touches lower band, second low is higher and doesn't touch band. Signals momentum shift.
bollingerBands.squeezeBreakoutDesc: When bands tighten significantly (low volatility), prepare for expansion. Trade the breakout in direction of the move with momentum confirmation.
bollingerBands.proTipTrending: The biggest mistake is fading band touches in trending markets. First identify if market is trending or ranging. Only use mean reversion in ranges; use band walks in trends.
bollingerBands.identifyingSqueezeP: A squeeze occurs when bands narrow to 6-month low width. This indicates extremely low volatility, which typically precedes a major move. The direction is unknown, but the move is coming.
bollingerBands.tradingBreakoutP: Wait for price to close outside the bands with expanding bands. Enter in direction of the breakout. The first expansion bar often sets the trend for days or weeks.
bollingerBands.momentumConfirmationP: Add the Keltner Channel. When Bollinger Bands are inside Keltner Channels, it's a squeeze. When they break outside, the squeeze is released. Trade in that direction.
bollingerBands.squeezeMistake1: Entering before the squeeze releases (premature entry)
bollingerBands.squeezeMistake2: Not using momentum indicator to confirm direction
bollingerBands.squeezeMistake4: Taking profits too early on squeeze breakouts
bollingerBands.riskMax: Don't trade bands in choppy, directionless markets
bollingerBands.mistake1: Treating band touch as automatic buy/sell signal
bollingerBands.mistake2: Ignoring whether market is trending or ranging
bollingerBands.mistake3: Using same strategy in all market conditions
bollingerBands.mistake4: Not confirming signals with volume or momentum
bollingerBands.mistake5: Setting stops too tight (within normal band range)
bollingerBands.bandwidthP: Bandwidth = (Upper Band - Lower Band) / Middle Band. This indicator quantifies the squeeze and helps identify historical low volatility points.
bollingerBands.bandwidthItem1: Track bandwidth over time — find 6-month lows
bollingerBands.bandwidthItem2: Historical low bandwidth often precedes major moves
bollingerBands.bandwidthItem3: Compare current bandwidth to historical average
bollingerBands.bandwidthItem4: Use bandwidth expansion to confirm breakouts
bollingerBands.proTipPercentB: Combine Bollinger Bands with %B indicator. %B shows where price is relative to the bands (0 = lower band, 1 = upper band, 0.5 = middle). This helps identify extreme readings and divergences more precisely.
bollingerBands.ctaDesc: Find squeeze setups and band trades with our scanner.

macd.title: MACD Trading Strategy: Mastering Signal Line Crossovers
macd.subtitle: The Moving Average Convergence Divergence (MACD) is one of the most reliable momentum indicators. Learn to trade crossovers, histogram analysis, and divergence setups.
macd.introAlert: MACD measures the relationship between two moving averages, revealing momentum shifts before they're visible in price alone. It's both a trend and momentum indicator.
macd.introP1: Created by Gerald Appel in the late 1970s, MACD has stood the test of time. It excels at identifying trend changes and momentum shifts. While simple to understand, mastering MACD requires understanding its nuances and knowing when NOT to trade its signals.
macd.chartCaption: MACD confirmation of trend continuation — histogram expanding with price movement.
macd.macdLineDesc: The difference between 12-period and 26-period EMAs. When MACD line is above zero, short-term momentum is bullish. Below zero, momentum is bearish.
macd.signalLineDesc: A 9-period EMA of the MACD line. It smooths the MACD and generates trading signals when crossed. Crossovers are the primary trading signals.
macd.histogramDesc: The difference between MACD and Signal lines, displayed as bars. Growing bars show increasing momentum; shrinking bars warn of momentum loss BEFORE the crossover.
macd.settingAdjust: Can adjust for sensitivity (8,17,9 faster; 19,39,9 slower)
macd.bullishCrossoverDesc: MACD line crosses above signal line. Enter long when crossover occurs, preferably near or below zero line for best risk/reward.
macd.bearishCrossoverDesc: MACD line crosses below signal line. Enter short or exit longs when crossover occurs, especially when near or above zero line.
macd.zeroLineCrossDesc: MACD crossing above zero confirms bullish trend; below zero confirms bearish. Use as trend filter — only take bullish signals above zero.
macd.histogramReversalDesc: Histogram shrinking warns of coming crossover. Prepare for trade when histogram starts shrinking after extended move.
macd.proTipHistogram: The histogram gives earlier signals than crossovers. When histogram starts shrinking but MACD hasn't crossed yet, it's an early warning. Prepare your entry rather than waiting for the crossover confirmation.
macd.bullishDivergenceP: Price makes lower lows but MACD makes higher lows. This shows selling pressure is weakening despite new price lows. Potential reversal signal.
macd.bearishDivergenceP: Price makes higher highs but MACD makes lower highs. Momentum is failing to confirm new highs. Warning of potential top.
macd.hiddenDivergenceP: In uptrend: price makes higher low but MACD makes lower low = trend continuation. In downtrend: price makes lower high but MACD makes higher high = continuation.
macd.divMistake1: Trading divergence in strong trends (can persist)
macd.divMistake2: Not waiting for price confirmation after divergence
macd.divMistake3: Using small divergence — look for significant divergence
macd.riskStopLoss: Below recent swing low or 1.5x ATR
macd.mistake1: Using MACD in sideways markets (many false signals)
macd.mistake2: Trading every crossover without additional confirmation
macd.mistake3: Ignoring the trend — MACD works best WITH trend
macd.mistake4: Using MACD alone without price action confirmation
macd.mistake5: Not adjusting settings for different market conditions
macd.multiTfP: Professional traders align MACD signals across multiple timeframes for higher probability trades.
macd.multiTfItem1: Weekly MACD above zero = bullish bias
macd.multiTfItem2: Daily MACD bullish crossover = entry signal
macd.multiTfItem4: Only take daily signals in direction of weekly MACD
macd.proTipRSI: Combine MACD with RSI for confirmation. When MACD gives bullish crossover AND RSI is above 50 (but not overbought), signal reliability increases significantly.
macd.ctaDesc: Scan for MACD crossover setups with our pattern scanner.

breakout.title: Breakout Trading: Capturing Explosive Moves
breakout.subtitle: Learn to identify and trade price breakouts from consolidation patterns, support/resistance levels, and chart formations — the moments when markets decide their next direction.
breakout.introAlert: A breakout occurs when price moves decisively beyond a defined level of support, resistance, or pattern boundary with increased momentum, signaling the start of a new trend or trend continuation.
breakout.introP1: Breakout trading capitalizes on the moment markets transition from consolidation to trending behavior. These explosive moves occur when accumulated buying or selling pressure finally overcomes a key level. The strategy is favored by momentum traders because breakouts often lead to sustained directional moves with favorable risk-reward ratios.
breakout.chartCaption: An ascending triangle breakout — price breaks above horizontal resistance after multiple tests, confirming bullish momentum.
breakout.horizontalDesc: Price breaks above resistance or below support that has been tested multiple times. The more tests, the more significant the breakout when it occurs.
breakout.chartPatternDesc: Breakouts from triangles, flags, wedges, and other formations. These often have measured move targets based on pattern height.
breakout.rangeDesc: Price breaks out of a defined trading range after consolidation. The longer the range, the more powerful the breakout tends to be.
breakout.volatilityDesc: Price moves beyond a volatility envelope (Bollinger Bands, ATR bands, Keltner Channels). These signal abnormal momentum regardless of specific price levels.
breakout.proTipTypes: Not all breakouts are equal. Breakouts from longer consolidation periods with multiple tests tend to be more reliable than breakouts from short, untested ranges.
breakout.identItem1: Level has been tested 2-4+ times (more tests = more significant)
breakout.identItem2: Price is consolidating in a tightening range before breakout
breakout.identItem4: The breakout candle has above-average volume
breakout.identItem5: Breakout occurs in direction of higher timeframe trend
breakout.identItem6: Price closes beyond the level (not just wicks)
breakout.identItem7: No major resistance immediately above breakout level
breakout.squeezeSetupP: The highest-probability breakouts occur after volatility contraction — when Bollinger Bands narrow inside Keltner Channels (the "TTM Squeeze"). This compression precedes explosive moves as pent-up energy is released.
breakout.aggressiveEntrySignalDesc: Enter immediately when price breaks above resistance (long) or below support (short) with a strong candle and increased volume. Use a buy-stop order at the breakout level.
breakout.aggressiveStopLossDesc: Place stop just below the breakout level (for longs) or just above (for shorts). If price falls back into the range, the breakout has failed.
breakout.aggressiveTargetDesc: Target 1.5-2x the height of the consolidation range, or the next significant resistance/support level.
breakout.conservativeEntrySignalDesc: Wait for the breakout to occur, then wait for price to pull back and retest the breakout level as new support/resistance. Enter on the bounce.
breakout.conservativeStopLossDesc: Place stop below the retest low (for longs) — typically 0.5-1x ATR below the breakout level.
breakout.conservativeTargetDesc: Same targets as aggressive entry, but with better risk-reward due to tighter stop.
breakout.tradeOffAlert: Aggressive entries catch more of the move but have lower win rates. Conservative retest entries have higher win rates but sometimes miss the move if no retest occurs.
breakout.falseBreakoutsP: Up to 50% of breakouts fail. False breakouts (also called "fakeouts" or "traps") occur when price breaks a level only to quickly reverse back into the range. Here's how to handle them:
breakout.lowVolBreakoutDesc: Breakout occurs on below-average volume — lack of conviction, likely to fail.
breakout.wickOnlyDesc: Price breaks level intraday but closes back inside — rejection signal.
breakout.immediateReversalDesc: Price reverses within 1-3 candles of breakout — trapped traders.
breakout.tradingFailedP: Failed breakouts can be excellent counter-trend opportunities:
breakout.tradingFailedItem1: If a bullish breakout fails and reverses, go short with stop above the false high
breakout.tradingFailedItem2: Target the opposite end of the range (old support)
breakout.tradingFailedItem3: These trades trap breakout traders, causing forced liquidation
breakout.commonMistake2: Using stops too tight (normal volatility triggers them)
breakout.commonMistake3: Chasing breakouts after they've already extended significantly
breakout.commonMistake4: Trading every breakout instead of waiting for A+ setups
breakout.commonMistake5: Not waiting for the candle close (entering on wicks)
breakout.commonMistake6: Ignoring the higher timeframe trend direction
breakout.multiTfP: The most reliable breakouts occur when multiple timeframes align. A daily breakout is more significant if the weekly chart also shows a breakout from a larger pattern.
breakout.orderFlowP: Professional traders use order flow data to validate breakouts:
breakout.deltaItem: Positive delta on bullish breakouts (more buying at ask)
breakout.largePrintsItem: Institutional orders appearing at breakout level
breakout.absorptionItem: Selling absorbed without price falling back
breakout.rectanglesTarget: Height of rectangle added to breakout point
breakout.trianglesTarget: Base width added to breakout point
breakout.flagsTarget: Length of flagpole added to breakout point
breakout.bestForItem2: Those comfortable with moderate win rates
breakout.bestForItem3: Patient traders who wait for setups
breakout.notIdealItem1: Those who can't handle false breakouts

dayTrading.title: Day Trading Strategy: Complete Intraday Guide
dayTrading.subtitle: Master the discipline of closing all positions before market close — capturing intraday volatility while sleeping soundly without overnight risk exposure.
dayTrading.introAlert: Day trading involves buying and selling securities within the same trading day. All positions are closed before the market closes, eliminating overnight gap risk.
dayTrading.introP1: Day trading offers a middle ground between the intensity of scalping and the patience required for swing trading. You have time to analyze setups properly, but still benefit from the immediate feedback loop of intraday trading. This strategy is popular among retail traders because it provides clear daily P&L and no overnight stress.
dayTrading.chartCaption: An ascending triangle breakout — a classic day trading setup on a 15-minute chart.
dayTrading.sameDayDesc: Every trade is opened and closed within the same trading session. This eliminates overnight gap risk and weekend exposure.
dayTrading.largerTargetsDesc: Day traders target 20-100+ pips per trade, allowing for better risk-reward ratios and more room for price to develop.
dayTrading.qualitySetupsDesc: Unlike scalping, day trading emphasizes waiting for high-probability setups. 3-10 well-chosen trades beat 50 mediocre ones.
dayTrading.capitalDesc: US pattern day trader rules require $25,000 minimum for stocks. Forex and futures have lower capital requirements.
dayTrading.proTipTiming: The best day trading opportunities occur during the first 2 hours after market open and the last hour before close. Mid-day sessions (11 AM - 2 PM EST) are often choppy and low-probability.
dayTrading.marketSelP: Not all markets are created equal for day trading. Choose instruments with the right characteristics for your style.
dayTrading.entryP: Master these core setups before adding complexity. Each setup has specific conditions that must be met for a valid trade.
dayTrading.orbEntryDesc: Wait for the first 15-30 minutes to establish an opening range. Enter on a decisive break above/below this range with increased volume.
dayTrading.orbStopDesc: Place stop at the opposite side of the opening range, or at the 50% level of the range for tighter risk.
dayTrading.orbTargetDesc: Target 1.5-2x the opening range height. Trail stop to breakeven after 1R profit.
dayTrading.vwapEntryDesc: In a trending day, wait for price to pull back to VWAP. Enter when price bounces off VWAP with a confirmation candle.
dayTrading.vwapStopDesc: Place stop just below VWAP (for longs) with a buffer of 2-3 ticks.
dayTrading.vwapTargetDesc: Target the high of the day (for longs) or a measured move equal to the distance from the day's low to VWAP.
dayTrading.failedEntryDesc: When price breaks a key level but quickly reverses back (within 2-3 candles), enter in the direction of the reversal. This traps breakout traders.
dayTrading.failedStopDesc: Place stop beyond the failed breakout's extreme point.
dayTrading.failedTargetDesc: Target the opposite end of the range or the next significant support/resistance level.
dayTrading.tradeMgmtP: How you manage trades in-flight determines long-term profitability more than entry accuracy.
dayTrading.scalingP: Many professional day traders scale out of positions in thirds:
dayTrading.scalingFirstDesc: Exit at 1R (1:1 risk-reward) to lock in profits
dayTrading.scalingSecondDesc: Exit at 2R with trailing stop at 1R
dayTrading.scalingThirdDesc: Let it run with trailing stop, targeting 3R+
dayTrading.mistake1: Moving stop loss further away when trade goes against you
dayTrading.mistake2: Closing winners too early and letting losers run (disposition effect)
dayTrading.mistake4: Trading during lunch hour when setups are low-probability
dayTrading.mistake5: Revenge trading after a loss to "make it back"
dayTrading.mistake6: Overtrading on slow days when there are no good setups
dayTrading.threeStrikeRuleDesc: If you hit 3 consecutive losses, stop trading for the day. This prevents emotional spiral trading and protects your capital for tomorrow.
dayTrading.multiTfP: Use the higher timeframe (60-min or daily) to determine bias, then execute on the lower timeframe (5-15 min). Trade in the direction of the higher timeframe trend.
dayTrading.marketInternalsP: Professional day traders monitor market internals for confirmation:
dayTrading.tickDesc: NYSE tick readings above +800 = bullish, below -800 = bearish
dayTrading.prepItem1: Review overnight price action and gaps
dayTrading.prepItem2: Check economic calendar for news events
dayTrading.prepItem4: Set daily bias (bullish, bearish, or neutral)
dayTrading.prepItem6: Set mental and physical state (hydrated, focused)
dayTrading.bestForItem1: Traders with 4+ hours of screen time daily
dayTrading.notIdealItem2: Those under $25K (US PDT rule for stocks)
dayTrading.notIdealItem4: Those who can't handle daily P&L swings
dayTrading.relatedScalpingDesc: Faster trades, tighter targets, more frequency

fibonacci.title: Fibonacci Retracement Strategy
fibonacci.subtitle: Master Fibonacci retracements and extensions to identify high-probability entry points and profit targets based on natural mathematical ratios.
fibonacci.understandingP: Fibonacci ratios are derived from the Fibonacci sequence where each number is the sum of the two preceding numbers (1, 1, 2, 3, 5, 8, 13, 21...). The key ratios used in trading are:
fibonacci.ratio786Desc: Deep retracement — last defense before reversal
fibonacci.drawingP: Proper Fibonacci placement is critical for accurate analysis:
fibonacci.drawUptrendDesc: Draw from swing low to swing high
fibonacci.drawDowntrendDesc: Draw from swing high to swing low
fibonacci.drawTip1: Use clear, significant swing points (not minor fluctuations)
fibonacci.drawTip2: Align with the dominant trend on higher timeframes
fibonacci.draw100P: The 100% level represents the starting point of the move; 0% is the endpoint.
fibonacci.goldenP: The zone between 61.8% and 65% is known as the "Golden Pocket" — the highest probability reversal area:
fibonacci.goldenEntryItem1: Identify a strong impulse move (trending leg)
fibonacci.goldenEntryItem2: Draw Fibonacci from swing low to high (uptrend) or vice versa
fibonacci.goldenEntryItem3: Wait for price to retrace into 61.8%-65% zone
fibonacci.shallowP: For strong trending markets, use the 38.2% level:
fibonacci.shallowEntryItem1: Confirm strong trend momentum (steep angle, small pullbacks)
fibonacci.shallowEntryItem3: Confirm with moving average confluence (21 EMA near 38.2%)
fibonacci.shallowEntryItem4: Enter on bullish/bearish engulfing at level
fibonacci.extensionsP: Use Fibonacci extensions to project profit targets beyond the original move:
fibonacci.stopInvalid: Invalid if price closes beyond 100% (original swing)
fibonacci.confluenceP: The most powerful setups occur when Fibonacci levels align with other technical factors:
fibonacci.checkItem1: Clear impulse move with defined swing points
fibonacci.checkItem2: Fibonacci level aligns with other S/R
fibonacci.multiTfP: Layer Fibonacci from different timeframes for confluence:
fibonacci.multiTfWeeklyDesc: Draw major Fib levels for context
fibonacci.multiTfDailyDesc: Identify current swing for trade Fib
fibonacci.multiTfCluster: When Fib levels from multiple timeframes align (within 0.5%), it creates a high-probability zone.
fibonacci.mistake1: Drawing Fibs on minor swings instead of significant moves
fibonacci.mistake2: Ignoring the trend direction when taking entries
fibonacci.mistake3: Not waiting for confirmation at Fib levels
fibonacci.mistake4: Using Fibs in choppy, ranging markets
fibonacci.mistake5: Expecting exact bounces (use zones, not lines)
fibonacci.marketForexDesc: Excellent on all major pairs — clear trends
fibonacci.marketCommoditiesDesc: Gold, Oil — Fibs work well with trends
fibonacci.marketCryptoDesc: BTC, ETH on 4H+ — good for swing trades

gapTrading.title: Gap Trading Strategy
gapTrading.subtitle: Learn to profit from price gaps — the high-probability setups that occur when markets open significantly higher or lower than the previous close.
gapTrading.whatIsP: A gap occurs when a security opens at a price significantly different from its previous close, leaving an empty space on the chart where no trading occurred.
gapTrading.gapUpDesc: Open price is above prior day's high
gapTrading.gapDownDesc: Open price is below prior day's low
gapTrading.fullGapDesc: Open outside prior day's entire range
gapTrading.partialGapDesc: Open beyond prior close but within range
gapTrading.typesP: Understanding gap types helps predict behavior:
gapTrading.commonGapsDesc: Small, occur in ranges — usually fill quickly (70%+ fill rate)
gapTrading.breakawayGapsDesc: Large, break key S/R — often don't fill; signal new trends
gapTrading.exhaustionGapsDesc: End of trend gaps — reversal imminent; fill quickly
gapTrading.fillP: Most gaps fill within the same session — capitalize on this tendency:
gapTrading.fillLongItem1: Identify gap down of 0.5-2% (too large = may not fill)
gapTrading.fillLongItem2: Wait 15-30 minutes for opening range to establish
gapTrading.fillLongItem3: Look for reversal signs: bullish candle, RSI oversold
gapTrading.fillLongItem5: Stop below the morning low (or opening range low)
gapTrading.fillShortItem3: Look for reversal: bearish candle, RSI overbought
gapTrading.gapGoP: Ride momentum when strong gaps extend rather than fill:
gapTrading.gapGoItem1: Identify gap with catalyst (earnings, news, sector move)
gapTrading.gapGoItem2: Gap size: 3%+ typically needed for continuation
gapTrading.gapGoItem3: Wait for first pullback after market open
gapTrading.gapGoItem4: Enter on break of first candle high (long) or low (short)
gapTrading.gapGoItem6: Target: Extension levels or prior resistance
gapTrading.orbP: Combine gap analysis with opening range:
gapTrading.orbItem1: Mark the first 15 or 30-minute range after open
gapTrading.orbItem6: Enter on break with stop at opposite range boundary
gapTrading.stopGoDesc: Below first pullback low (longs) / above high (shorts)
gapTrading.stopORBDesc: Opposite side of the opening range
gapTrading.stopMax: Max risk: 1% account on gap trades (volatile)
gapTrading.tpGo1Desc: Measured move (gap size projected from entry)
gapTrading.tpGo2Desc: Prior swing high/low or key resistance
gapTrading.filtersP: Increase win rate by filtering setups:
gapTrading.filterVolumeDesc: Gap on above-average volume = more reliable
gapTrading.filterCatalystDesc: News-driven gaps more likely to run
gapTrading.filterTrendDesc: Trade gaps in direction of larger trend
gapTrading.filterSRDesc: Gap into major S/R often reverses
gapTrading.checkItem1: Gap size appropriate for strategy (fill vs. continuation)
gapTrading.checkItem6: Gap direction aligns with sector/market trend
gapTrading.preItem4Desc: Mark prior day's high/low/close, key S/R
gapTrading.preItem5Desc: Determine strategy (fill vs. go) before open
gapTrading.mistake1: Trading immediately at the open (wait for range)
gapTrading.mistake3: Ignoring the catalyst behind the gap
gapTrading.mistake4: Using too large position sizes on volatile opens
gapTrading.mistake5: Holding gap fills too long (take profit at prior close)

meanReversion.title: Mean Reversion: Trading the Return to Average
meanReversion.subtitle: Exploit the statistical tendency of prices to revert to their historical average. Buy oversold conditions, sell overbought — the contrarian's edge in ranging markets.
meanReversion.introAlert: Mean reversion is based on the statistical observation that extreme price moves tend to revert toward the average over time. Buy when price is below average, sell when above.
meanReversion.introP1: While trend followers bet on momentum continuing, mean reversion traders bet on momentum exhausting. This counter-trend approach works best in range-bound, mean-reverting markets and is the foundation of many quantitative hedge fund strategies. The key is identifying when a move is "stretched" and likely to snap back.
meanReversion.chartCaption: A double bottom showing mean reversion — price falls to an extreme, finds support, and reverts toward the mean.
meanReversion.theoryBoxTitle: Why Prices Revert to the Mean
meanReversion.theoryStatDesc: Random fluctuations around a true value will, on average, return to that value over time.
meanReversion.theoryProfitDesc: Extreme moves attract profit-taking from existing holders, reversing momentum.
meanReversion.theoryValueDesc: Extreme prices attract value-oriented traders who fade the move.
meanReversion.theoryMicroDesc: Market makers fade extreme moves to provide liquidity and capture spread.
meanReversion.theoryWarningDesc: Mean reversion FAILS in trending markets. Trading reversion during a strong trend is a common cause of blown accounts. Always confirm the market is range-bound before applying this strategy.
meanReversion.bbDesc: Price at the upper band = overbought, lower band = oversold. Mean reversion targets the middle band (20 SMA). Works best when bands are contracting (low volatility).
meanReversion.rsiDesc: RSI below 30 = oversold, above 70 = overbought. For mean reversion, use RSI(2) for more sensitive readings. Buy when RSI(2) < 10, sell when RSI(2) > 90.
meanReversion.distanceDesc: Measure how far price has deviated from a moving average (e.g., 20 SMA). Extreme deviations (2+ standard deviations) signal potential reversion.
meanReversion.zScoreDesc: Z-Score = (Price - Mean) / Standard Deviation. Z-Score above +2 = overbought, below -2 = oversold. More statistically rigorous than simple indicators.
meanReversion.proTipRSI: The 2-period RSI (RSI-2) is one of the most powerful mean reversion indicators. Research by Larry Connors shows that buying when RSI-2 drops below 5 and selling when it rises above 95 generates significant edge in equity indices.
meanReversion.rsi2LongDesc: Buy when RSI(2) closes below 5 AND price is above the 200 MA (confirms we're in an overall uptrend).
meanReversion.rsi2ExitDesc: Sell when RSI(2) closes above 70 OR after 5 days, whichever comes first.
meanReversion.rsi2StopDesc: Exit if price closes below a significant swing low or 3% below entry.
meanReversion.bb2LongDesc: Buy when price closes below the lower Bollinger Band (20,2) and shows a bullish reversal candle (hammer, bullish engulfing).
meanReversion.bb2TargetDesc: Target the middle band (20 SMA) or the upper band for extended moves.
meanReversion.bb2StopDesc: Place stop below the swing low or 1-1.5x ATR below entry.
meanReversion.multiDayLongDesc: After 3-5 consecutive down days in an uptrend (price above 200 MA), buy on the first up close.
meanReversion.multiDayExitDesc: Exit after 3-5 days or when price reaches the 10-day high.
meanReversion.exitTimeDesc: Exit after a fixed number of days (3-10 days) regardless of profit. Mean reversion is a short-term edge that decays quickly.
meanReversion.exitTargetDesc: Exit when price returns to the mean (moving average) or when the overbought/oversold indicator returns to neutral (RSI 50).
meanReversion.exitIndicatorDesc: Exit when the indicator reaches the opposite extreme. If you bought on RSI < 10, sell on RSI > 90.
meanReversion.mistake1: Fighting strong trends (just because it's overbought doesn't mean it will reverse)
meanReversion.mistake2: Averaging down on losers (turns small losses into catastrophic ones)
meanReversion.mistake3: Holding positions too long (mean reversion edge decays quickly)
meanReversion.mistake4: Using fixed profit targets that are too ambitious
meanReversion.mistake5: Not filtering for overall market regime (trend vs. range)
meanReversion.mistake6: Trading mean reversion without stop losses
meanReversion.avgDownWarningDesc: The biggest risk in mean reversion is thinking "it's even more oversold now, I'll add more." This can lead to massive losses when an extreme move continues. NEVER add to losing positions.
meanReversion.regimeP: Only trade mean reversion when the market is in a ranging regime:
meanReversion.regimeADXDesc: Weak trend = good for mean reversion
meanReversion.regimeOff: Turn off mean reversion when ADX > 30
meanReversion.pairsP: Trade the spread between two correlated instruments (e.g., Coca-Cola vs. PepsiCo). When the spread deviates from its historical mean, bet on convergence. This is market-neutral and doesn't depend on overall direction.
meanReversion.intradayP: Fade extreme moves during the trading day:
meanReversion.intradayItem1: Trade against gap opens that are >2% from previous close
meanReversion.intradayItem2: VWAP reversion: Price extended from VWAP tends to return
meanReversion.intradayItem3: Opening range: Fade breaks of opening range during lunch hours
meanReversion.checkItem1: Confirm market is in ranging regime (ADX < 25)
meanReversion.checkItem3: Price is still above 200 MA for longs (or below for shorts)
meanReversion.checkItem6: Position size calculated based on stop distance
meanReversion.bestForItem3: Those who prefer higher win rates

momentum.title: Momentum Trading: Riding Strong Price Movements
momentum.subtitle: Capture accelerating price action by identifying and trading assets showing strong directional momentum. A favorite strategy among swing traders and active investors.
momentum.introAlert: Momentum trading is based on the premise that strong price movements tend to continue. "The trend is your friend" — ride the wave until momentum exhausts.
momentum.introP: Momentum traders buy assets that are rising and sell those that are falling, betting that the current trend will persist. This strategy exploits the behavioral tendency of markets to trend — the "herding effect" where traders pile into winning positions.
momentum.chartCaption: Strong momentum continuation after breakout — the ideal momentum entry scenario.
momentum.rsiDesc: Measures speed and magnitude of price changes. RSI above 50 indicates bullish momentum, below 50 bearish. Strong momentum: RSI 60-70 for longs, 30-40 for shorts.
momentum.macdDesc: Shows the difference between MACD line and signal line. Growing histogram bars indicate increasing momentum; shrinking bars signal momentum loss.
momentum.rocDesc: Measures percentage change over a period. Rising ROC confirms acceleration; falling ROC warns of deceleration even if price is still rising.
momentum.check1: Price above key moving averages (20/50 EMA)
momentum.check3: MACD histogram expanding in trade direction
momentum.check5: Higher highs and higher lows forming
momentum.pullbackEntryDesc: Enter on pullbacks to moving averages during strong momentum. Wait for price to touch 20 EMA and show rejection candle before entering.
momentum.breakoutEntryDesc: Enter after price breaks above resistance with strong volume. Momentum continuation often follows consolidation near highs.
momentum.rsi50EntryDesc: In strong uptrends, RSI often bounces off the 50 level. Enter when RSI touches 50 and turns back up with price confirmation.
momentum.proTipEntry: The best momentum entries occur when multiple timeframes align. A daily uptrend with a 4H pullback completing offers the highest probability setup.
momentum.trailingStopsDesc: Use ATR-based trailing stops to lock in profits while allowing room for normal volatility. A 2x ATR trail from swing highs keeps you in trending moves.
momentum.scalingOutDesc: Take partial profits at key resistance levels. Sell 1/3 at first target, 1/3 at second, trail the final 1/3 for potential extended moves.
momentum.exhaustionSignsDesc: Exit when you see bearish divergence (price making new highs while RSI/MACD make lower highs), volume drying up on advances, or climactic volume spikes.
momentum.riskStopLoss: Below recent swing low or 2x ATR
momentum.riskMax: 5% total portfolio risk across all positions
momentum.mistake1: Chasing extended moves — enter on pullbacks, not at highs
momentum.relStrengthP: Professional momentum traders compare assets within sectors to find relative strength leaders. A stock outperforming its sector during market pullbacks often leads the next leg up.
momentum.relStrength1: Compare asset performance to benchmark (SPY, sector ETF)
momentum.relStrength2: Look for assets making new highs when market is flat
momentum.relStrength3: Track relative strength line breakouts before price breakouts
momentum.relStrength4: Rotate into strongest performers within hot sectors
momentum.proTipInstitutional: Institutional traders use "momentum factor" screens that combine price momentum, earnings momentum, and estimate revisions. Stocks ranking high across all three dimensions show the strongest continuation.
momentum.ctaDesc: Practice identifying momentum setups with our pattern recognition tools.

positionTrading.title: Position Trading: Building Wealth Through Major Trends
positionTrading.subtitle: Hold positions for weeks to months, capturing major market trends while minimizing transaction costs and time spent monitoring. The approach of legendary investors.
positionTrading.introAlert: Position trading bridges the gap between active trading and long-term investing. It combines technical timing with fundamental conviction to capture major market moves.
positionTrading.introP: Position traders think in terms of months, not days. They aim to capture 20-100%+ moves by identifying major trends early and holding through normal volatility. This approach requires patience and strong conviction, but offers potentially life-changing returns.
positionTrading.chartCaption: A major base formation — the type of pattern position traders seek for multi-month holds.
positionTrading.earningsDesc: Look for companies with accelerating earnings growth — 25%+ year-over-year growth with acceleration in recent quarters. Earnings drive long-term stock performance.
positionTrading.revenueDesc: Revenue growth validates earnings quality. Companies growing revenue 15%+ with expanding margins have the fundamentals to support extended price advances.
positionTrading.industryDesc: Focus on market leaders within growing industries. The #1 or #2 player in a growing sector often captures disproportionate gains as the sector expands.
positionTrading.check2: Revenue growth 15%+ with stable or improving margins
positionTrading.check4: Strong market position (#1 or #2 in niche)
positionTrading.baseBreakoutDesc: Enter when price breaks out of multi-month base (flat base, cup-with-handle, double bottom) on heavy volume. The longer the base, the bigger the potential move.
positionTrading.weeklyMADesc: In established uptrends, buy pullbacks to the 10-week or 40-week moving average. These often mark excellent add points.
positionTrading.sectorRotationDesc: Enter when money flows into a neglected sector. Early sector rotation signals often precede major moves in individual names.
positionTrading.proTipTiming: The best position trades occur when strong fundamentals align with a clear technical breakout. Either alone is good; both together is exceptional.
positionTrading.concentratedDesc: Position traders typically hold 5-15 positions. Too many dilutes returns; too few increases risk. Find your balance based on conviction levels and capital.
positionTrading.positionSizingDesc: Start with 5-10% positions; add to winners as they prove themselves. Your best ideas should become your biggest positions through pyramiding, not initial sizing.
positionTrading.sectorAllocDesc: Limit exposure to any single sector to 30-40%. Sector concentration is fine when that sector is leading, but diversify to protect against sector rotation.
positionTrading.riskStopLoss: 7-8% below entry or below key weekly support
positionTrading.riskMax: 25% total portfolio in any single sector
positionTrading.mistake1: Selling too early — big moves take time to develop
positionTrading.mistake2: Averaging down on losing positions (hoping for recovery)
positionTrading.mistake4: Not cutting losses when thesis breaks down
positionTrading.mistake5: Excessive concentration in single stock or sector
positionTrading.pyramidingP: Professional position traders add to winning positions as they prove themselves. This concentrates capital in what's working while limiting initial risk.
positionTrading.pyramid2: First add: After 2-5% profit, add 25% more
positionTrading.pyramid3: Second add: At next buy point (pullback to support), final 25%
positionTrading.pyramid4: Never add to losing positions — only winners
positionTrading.proTipSitting: The biggest position trading gains come from sitting. Jesse Livermore said, "It was never my thinking that made the big money, it was my sitting." Have patience with winners.
positionTrading.ctaDesc: Find major base breakouts and trend leaders with our scanner.

rsiDivergence.title: RSI Divergence Strategy
rsiDivergence.subtitle: Master the art of identifying trend reversals using RSI divergence patterns — one of the most reliable momentum-based signals in technical analysis.
rsiDivergence.whatIsP1: RSI (Relative Strength Index) divergence occurs when price action and the RSI indicator move in opposite directions, signaling potential trend exhaustion and imminent reversal.
rsiDivergence.whatIsP2: There are two primary types of divergence:
rsiDivergence.bullishDivDesc: Price makes lower lows while RSI makes higher lows — signals potential upward reversal
rsiDivergence.bearishDivDesc: Price makes higher highs while RSI makes lower highs — signals potential downward reversal
rsiDivergence.whatIsP3: Divergence works because momentum often leads price. When the RSI fails to confirm new price extremes, it suggests underlying buying or selling pressure is weakening.
rsiDivergence.settingsP: Standard RSI settings work well for divergence trading:
rsiDivergence.settingsNote: For divergence specifically, the exact levels matter less than the slope comparison between price and RSI.
rsiDivergence.bullEntry2: RSI forms a higher low (divergence confirmed)
rsiDivergence.bullEntry4: Wait for bullish price confirmation (engulfing candle, break of mini-resistance)
rsiDivergence.bullEntry5: Enter long on confirmation candle close
rsiDivergence.bearEntry2: RSI forms a lower high (divergence confirmed)
rsiDivergence.bearEntry4: Wait for bearish price confirmation (bearish engulfing, break of mini-support)
rsiDivergence.bearEntry5: Enter short on confirmation candle close
rsiDivergence.stop1Desc: Stop below the lowest low of the divergence pattern
rsiDivergence.stop2Desc: Stop above the highest high of the divergence pattern
rsiDivergence.stop3: Add 0.5-1 ATR buffer for volatility protection
rsiDivergence.tp3Desc: Trail with 20 EMA or until RSI reaches opposite extreme
rsiDivergence.quality1: Clear swing points visible on both price and RSI
rsiDivergence.quality2: RSI in oversold/overbought zone during divergence
rsiDivergence.quality3: Divergence forms over 10-50 bars (not too fast, not too slow)
rsiDivergence.quality5: Higher timeframe trend supports reversal direction
rsiDivergence.hiddenP1: Hidden divergence signals trend continuation rather than reversal:
rsiDivergence.hiddenBullishDesc: Price makes higher lows, RSI makes lower lows — buy in uptrend
rsiDivergence.hiddenBearishDesc: Price makes lower highs, RSI makes higher highs — sell in downtrend
rsiDivergence.hiddenP2: Hidden divergence is particularly powerful when trading pullbacks within established trends.
rsiDivergence.mtfP: Increase accuracy by analyzing multiple timeframes:
rsiDivergence.mtfExample: Example: Daily trend bullish → 4H bullish divergence forms → 1H for precise entry.
rsiDivergence.mistake3: Using too short RSI periods (creates noise)
rsiDivergence.mistake4: Trading divergence during strong momentum trends
rsiDivergence.marketForexDesc: Major pairs with good liquidity (EUR/USD, GBP/USD)
rsiDivergence.marketStocksDesc: Large-cap stocks with smooth price action

scalping.title: Scalping Strategy: Profiting from Small Price Movements
scalping.subtitle: Master the art of rapid-fire trading — capturing dozens of small profits throughout the day that compound into substantial returns. Used by proprietary trading firms and professional day traders worldwide.
scalping.introAlert: Scalping is an ultra-short-term trading strategy that aims to profit from small price changes. Scalpers execute many trades per day, each targeting tiny profits that accumulate over time.
scalping.introP: Scalping requires intense focus, quick decision-making, and flawless execution. It's not for everyone — but for those with the right temperament and setup, it offers a way to generate consistent daily income without overnight risk exposure. This strategy is favored by proprietary trading firms due to its predictable risk profile and ability to compound small edges.
scalping.chartCaption: A typical scalping opportunity — quick entry on momentum with tight stop loss and small profit target.
scalping.holdingDesc: Positions are held for seconds to minutes — rarely more than 15 minutes. The goal is to capture quick moves and exit before the market can reverse.
scalping.profitDesc: Each trade targets 5-20 pips (forex) or 0.1-0.5% moves. The edge comes from consistency and volume, not individual trade size.
scalping.volumeDesc: Scalpers may execute 20-100+ trades per day. This volume requires low commissions and tight spreads to remain profitable.
scalping.riskControlDesc: Stop losses are extremely tight — often just 3-10 pips. One large loss can wipe out many small wins, so discipline is paramount.
scalping.proTipLiquidity: Scalping works best in liquid markets with tight spreads. Forex majors (EUR/USD, GBP/USD), large-cap stocks, and major index futures are ideal. Avoid illiquid instruments where spread costs eat into profits.
scalping.setupP: Scalping demands more from your trading infrastructure than any other style. Cutting corners on setup will cost you money.
scalping.setup1: Direct market access (DMA) broker with sub-millisecond execution
scalping.setup2: Commission structure of $1 or less per round trip
scalping.setup3: Spreads under 1 pip for forex, 1 cent for stocks
scalping.setup6: Keyboard shortcuts or hotkeys for rapid order entry
scalping.costWarningDesc: If your broker charges $5+ per trade, scalping becomes nearly impossible to profit from. Commission costs can exceed 50% of gross profits for frequent traders with the wrong broker.
scalping.entryP: Scalpers use several entry techniques, often combining multiple signals for confirmation.
scalping.momentumEntryDesc: Enter in the direction of strong momentum when price breaks above a short-term high (long) or below a short-term low (short) with increased volume. Use 1-minute or 5-minute charts.
scalping.momentumStopDesc: Place stop 3-5 pips behind the entry bar's low (for longs) or high (for shorts). Maximum risk per trade: 0.1-0.25% of account.
scalping.momentumTargetDesc: Target 1:1 to 1.5:1 reward-to-risk. Exit when momentum stalls or at the next micro resistance/support level.
scalping.rangeEntryDesc: During consolidation periods, buy at range support and sell at range resistance. Best during low-volatility sessions (Asian session for forex).
scalping.rangeStopDesc: Place stop just outside the range boundary — 2-3 pips beyond support/resistance.
scalping.rangeTargetDesc: Target 70-80% of the range width. Don't try to capture the entire range.
scalping.orderFlowEntryDesc: Monitor DOM (Depth of Market) for large orders being absorbed. Enter when significant buying/selling pressure is visible but price hasn't moved yet.
scalping.orderFlowStopDesc: Exit immediately if the large order is pulled or absorbed without price movement.
scalping.orderFlowTargetDesc: Quick 3-5 pip scalp as price adjusts to the order flow imbalance.
scalping.exitP: In scalping, exits matter more than entries. A perfect entry with poor exit management will still lose money over time.
scalping.tp1: Use limit orders at predetermined targets — never chase
scalping.tp2: Consider scaling out: 50% at first target, 50% at extended target
scalping.tp3: Exit immediately when momentum stalls (price clusters)
scalping.tp4: Never turn a scalp into a day trade — stick to your timeframe
scalping.cl1: Hard stops are non-negotiable — never move a stop further away
scalping.cl2: Exit early if trade thesis is invalidated (even before stop)
scalping.cl3: Accept small losses as a cost of doing business
scalping.exitMistake1: Holding losers hoping they'll recover (turning scalps into day trades)
scalping.exitMistake2: Taking profits too early out of fear, then watching price run
scalping.exitMistake3: Overtrading after losses to "make it back"
scalping.exitMistake4: Ignoring spread costs when calculating profitability
scalping.exitMistake5: Trading during news events without adjusting position size
scalping.mathP: With a 1:1 risk-reward ratio and 60% win rate:
scalping.proP: These advanced techniques separate profitable scalpers from the rest.
scalping.todAvoidDesc: Lunch hours (12-2 PM EST) — low volume, choppy action
scalping.corrP: Watch correlated instruments for leading signals. EUR/USD often leads USD/CHF inversely. ES futures can signal SPY moves. Use correlations to confirm entries or spot divergences.
scalping.detachmentDesc: Treat each trade as one of thousands. No single trade matters — only the statistical edge over time.
scalping.routineDesc: Same setup time, same pre-market analysis, same warm-up trades. Consistency in routine breeds consistency in results.
scalping.healthDesc: Sleep 7+ hours. Exercise daily. Hydrate. Poor physical condition = poor decision-making under pressure.
scalping.bestFor2: Those who prefer many small wins
scalping.notIdeal3: Traders who need time to think
scalping.notIdeal4: Those who can't handle rapid losses
scalping.relatedDayTradingDesc: Longer holds, fewer trades, same intraday principle
scalping.relatedBreakoutDesc: Key level breaks that scalpers exploit

agentScoring.title: Agent Scoring
agentScoring.subtitle: Proof-gated scoring — only signals with a proven historical edge (≥15 trades, ≥45% win rate) are scored. Unproven signals can be expanded via the Emerging toggle.
agentScoring.presetTooltipDesc: Each preset adjusts how the 4 AI agents are weighted when scoring trades.
agentScoring.analystTooltip: Bayesian win-probability & historical hit-rates for the detected pattern. Higher weight → prioritize setups with strong statistical edge.
agentScoring.riskTooltip: ATR-based stop placement, Kelly sizing & risk/reward quality. Higher weight → prioritize tight risk control with well-defined stops.
agentScoring.timingTooltip: Blends trend alignment (with_trend=0.85, neutral=0.55, counter=0.30) 50/50 with live economic calendar data. Penalizes entries near high-impact events (−0.15 per FOMC/NFP/CPI) and medium-impact (−0.06) within 48h, matched by currency to your instrument. Higher weight → avoid trading into scheduled volatility.
agentScoring.portfolioTooltip: Evaluates real portfolio exposure when basket symbols are selected: asset-class concentration, currency correlation (e.g. AUD/USD + NZD/USD overlap), and directional skew. Without basket context, falls back to pattern quality grade. Higher weight → enforce diversification.
agentScoring.addSymbolsPlaceholder: Add symbols or pick from table +
agentScoring.scoresUpdateLive: — scores update live as you adjust agents
agentScoring.noDetections: No active detections for this filter combination.
agentScoring.noDetectionsHint: Try changing the asset class or timeframe filter.
agentScoring.rrTooltip: Risk-to-Reward ratio. How much potential profit per unit of risk. Higher is better — 2.0+ is the baseline.
agentScoring.analystHeaderTooltip: 3-component score: Win Rate (0–10 pts), Expectancy R-multiple (0–10 pts), and Sample Confidence (0–5 pts, log₂ scale). Only scored for proven signals (≥15 trades, ≥45% win rate).
agentScoring.riskHeaderTooltip: 3-component score: R:R Adequacy (0–10 pts, 1.5 R:R = 5 pts), Volatility Stability (0–8 pts, tighter stops = higher), and Kelly Sizing (0–7 pts, capped at 25%). Evaluates position sizing feasibility.
agentScoring.timingHeaderTooltip: 50% trend alignment (with_trend=0.85, neutral=0.55, counter=0.30 via EMA, MACD, RSI, ADX) + 50% economic event calendar. High-impact events (−0.15 each) and medium (−0.06) within 48h for the instrument's currencies reduce this score. Clear calendar + with-trend = highest score.
agentScoring.portfolioHeaderTooltip: When basket has symbols: real concentration risk (asset-class overlap), currency correlation (shared USD/EUR/JPY exposure), and directional skew (too many longs/shorts). Without basket: quality grade proxy. Add symbols to basket for meaningful portfolio scoring.
agentScoring.scoreTooltip: Weighted composite score (0–100) combining all four agent scores. Only calculated for proven signals that pass the Proof Gate (≥15 historical trades with ≥45% win rate). Emerging signals are not scored.
agentScoring.verdictTooltip: TAKE (≥70) = actionable edge, WATCH (50–69) = monitor, SKIP (<50) = pass. Only proven signals receive a verdict. Signals without sufficient history are labeled UNPROVEN and separated into the Emerging section.
agentScoring.basketTooltip: Add symbols to the backtest basket. Selected symbols will auto-populate the Run Backtest input.
agentScoring.takeRateTooltip: Percentage of scanned setups that scored ≥70 (TAKE verdict). A high Take Rate means your current weights are accepting more trades — good for aggressive styles, but watch for quality dilution.
agentScoring.watchRateTooltip: Percentage of setups scoring 50–69 (WATCH verdict). These are borderline opportunities worth monitoring but not yet actionable. A high Watch Rate suggests many setups are close to your threshold.
agentScoring.skipRateTooltip: Percentage of setups scoring below 50 (SKIP verdict). A high Skip Rate means your agents are filtering aggressively — typical of conservative presets. This protects capital but may miss opportunities.
agentScoring.avgScoreTooltip: The mean composite score (0–100) across all scanned setups, weighted by your agent configuration. Compare this across presets to see how your weighting strategy shifts overall market sentiment.
agentScoring.addSymbolsFirst: Add symbols to the basket first. Click + next to rows in the table.
agentScoring.tooManyInstruments: Too many instruments ({{count}}). Maximum is {{max}} per run. Remove some from the basket.
agentScoring.noActivePatterns: No active patterns found for selected symbols. Pick symbols from the table.

agentScoring.authGateDesc: You've used your free backtest. Sign in or create a free account to unlock more backtests and save your results.
agentScoring.authGateFreeDesc: Agent Scoring is free to use. Create a free account to run your first backtest and validate your trade ideas with historical data.
agentScoring.faqWhatAreDesc: The Multi-Agent Trade Filter uses four specialized AI agents that independently evaluate every trade opportunity. Before scoring, each signal must pass the Proof Gate — only setups backed by ≥15 historical trades with a ≥45% win rate are scored. Signals that don't meet this threshold are labeled UNPROVEN and shown in a separate Emerging Signals section. Agent Weights control how much influence each agent has on the final Composite Score (0–100) that determines whether a proven trade is labeled TAKE, WATCH, or SKIP.
agentScoring.faqProofGateDesc: The Proof Gate ensures you only act on signals with a verified statistical edge. A signal must have: (1) at least 15 resolved historical trades for that pattern + instrument combination, and (2) a win rate of 45% or higher. Signals failing either criterion are classified as Emerging — they are still visible but not scored, and their Analyst and Composite columns show '—' with an UNPROVEN badge. Send emerging signals to Pattern Lab to investigate their potential.
agentScoring.faqFormulaDesc: The four weights must sum to 100. Each agent's raw score (0–1) is multiplied by its weight, and the results are summed to produce the composite. A composite of 70+ defaults to TAKE, 50–69 to WATCH, and below 50 to SKIP (thresholds are adjustable). Only proven signals (those passing the Proof Gate) receive a composite score.

ascending-triangle.name: Ascending Triangle
ascending-triangle.description: A bullish continuation pattern formed by a flat resistance line and a rising support trendline, indicating increasing buying pressure.

descending-triangle.name: Descending Triangle
descending-triangle.description: A bearish continuation pattern with a flat support and declining resistance, suggesting sellers are gaining control.

symmetrical-triangle.name: Symmetrical Triangle
symmetrical-triangle.description: A neutral consolidation pattern with converging trendlines that can break in either direction, reflecting market indecision.

double-bottom.name: Double Bottom
double-bottom.description: A bullish reversal pattern that forms after a downtrend when price tests a support level twice and bounces, resembling the letter "W".

double-top.name: Double Top
double-top.description: A bearish reversal pattern where price tests a resistance level twice and fails to break through, forming an "M" shape.

triple-bottom.name: Triple Bottom
triple-bottom.description: A bullish reversal pattern with three roughly equal lows, indicating strong support and potential trend reversal.

triple-top.name: Triple Top
triple-top.description: A bearish reversal pattern where price tests resistance three times, signaling exhaustion and a likely downside break.

head-and-shoulders.name: Head and Shoulders
head-and-shoulders.description: A classic bearish reversal pattern with three peaks — the middle peak (head) is the highest, flanked by two lower peaks (shoulders).

inverse-head-and-shoulders.name: Inverse Head and Shoulders
inverse-head-and-shoulders.description: A bullish reversal pattern mirroring the head and shoulders, with three troughs where the middle is the deepest.

bull-flag.name: Bull Flag
bull-flag.description: A short-term bullish continuation pattern where a sharp upward move is followed by a brief rectangular consolidation before continuing higher.

bear-flag.name: Bear Flag
bear-flag.description: A bearish continuation pattern where a sharp decline is followed by a brief upward-sloping consolidation before resuming lower.

rising-wedge.name: Rising Wedge
rising-wedge.description: A bearish pattern where price converges within upward-sloping trendlines, typically breaking down as buying momentum fades.

falling-wedge.name: Falling Wedge
falling-wedge.description: A bullish pattern with downward-converging trendlines, usually resolving to the upside as selling pressure diminishes.

cup-and-handle.name: Cup & Handle
cup-and-handle.description: A bullish continuation pattern resembling a teacup, with a rounded bottom (cup) followed by a slight pullback (handle) before breakout.

inverse-cup-and-handle.name: Inverse Cup & Handle
inverse-cup-and-handle.description: A bearish variant of the cup and handle, with a rounded top followed by a small rally before breaking down.

donchian-breakout-long.name: Donchian Breakout (Long)
donchian-breakout-long.description: A momentum-based long signal triggered when price breaks above the upper Donchian channel, indicating a potential new uptrend.

donchian-breakout-short.name: Donchian Breakout (Short)
donchian-breakout-short.description: A momentum-based short signal triggered when price breaks below the lower Donchian channel, suggesting a potential new downtrend.
